title: Direct Time-Domain Observation of l-Doubling via Centrifugal-Distortion Pre-compensation
authors: Inbar Sternbach, Kfir Rutman Moshe, Amit Beer, Soumitra Hazra, Sharly Fleischer
abstract: We demonstrate direct time-domain observation of l-doubling contributions in molecular rotational dynamics using shaped femtosecond laser pulses. By imposing a tailored spectral phase on the excitation pulse, we pre-compensate centrifugal distortion, which otherwise leads to temporally broadened, multi-cycle revival structures that obscure fine rotational features. A cubic spectral phase [Phys. Rev. A 107, 053108 (2023)] compresses selected revivals into near single-cycle events, in agreement with an analytic expression derived from molecular rotational constants, enabling predictive pulse design beyond numerical optimization. The resulting distortion-free revivals reveal temporally separated l-doubling contributions that remain unresolved in conventional impulsive alignment experiments. The method proves robust against experimental imperfections, including spatial light modulator discretization. While selective control of individual l-doubling components becomes feasible, here we focus on their direct observation in the time domain.
summary: This paper presents a method to use shaped femtosecond pulses to cancel out centrifugal distortion in rotating molecules. By pre-compensating for this distortion, the researchers were able to resolve fine rotational features, such as $l$-doubling, that are normally obscured in time-domain experiments.
main_problem: Centrifugal distortion in molecular rotational dynamics causes $J$-dependent dephasing, which leads to broadened, multi-cycle revival structures that obscure fine rotational features like $l$-doubling.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that applying a cubic spectral phase to femtosecond pulses can pre-compensate for centrifugal distortion, compressing distorted revivals into near single-cycle events and enabling the direct time-domain observation of $l$-doubling in CO2.
method: The study uses a 4-f pulse shaper with a Spatial Light Modulator to impose a tailored cubic spectral phase on an ultrashort laser pulse, based on an analytical approximation for the required phase coefficient.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the rotational dynamics of linear molecules, specifically CH3I for validating centrifugal distortion compensation and CO2 for observing $l$-doubling. The molecules are modeled as rigid rotors with a $J^4$ centrifugal distortion correction.
key_observables: Time-dependent molecular alignment (induced birefringence) and rotational revival structures.
important_parameters: Rotational constants (B, D), cubic spectral phase coefficients (b-tilde), and gas temperature (up to 403 K for CO2).
main_assumptions: The model neglects the linear term in J (time shift) and the J-independent global phase, focusing primarily on quadratic and cubic spectral phase terms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup for transient birefringence; Figure 2 demonstrates the compression of distorted CH3I alignment signals using shaped pulses; Figure 3 shows the resolution of $l$-doubling in CO2 through distortion mitigation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of centrifugal distortion, presents an analytical framework for pulse design, validates the method using CH3I, and demonstrates the observation of $l$-doubling in CO2.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and AMO physics as it demonstrates precise coherent control over rotational wavepackets, providing a method to resolve fine-scale quantum features that are otherwise hidden by dephasing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Transient birefringence setup. 𝑃 – polarizer, L- lens, λ/2-half wave plate, λ/4 – quarter wave plate, BBO crystal, WP- Wollaston prism, TS – Translation stage, F – filter (BG40), PD- Potodetector. The pump beam is routed through a pulse shaper, 4-f setup where G-grating, Cyl M are cylindrical reflective lenses and SLM- Spatial Light Modulator (dual mask, 640 pixels, Jenoptik).

Figure 2 (a) Time-resolved birefringence signal from CH3I gas (15 torr, room temperature) excited by a 120fs, 800nm pulse (without phase shaping). Figs.(b,c,d) enlarged view of the 1.5, 2.5, 5.5 revival signals respectively. Black signals were induced by a transform limited pulse (unshaped, as in fig.2a) and blue signals were induced by cubic-phase-shaped pulses with b̃ values noted in the figures. Figs.(e,f,g) show the simulation results for (b,c,d) respectively.

Figure 2a depicts the time-resolved alignment signal of CH3I following excitation by a transform-limited (unshaped) near-IR pulse with ~120 fs duration. The effects of CDN on the shape of the revival signals manifests as gradual increase in the number of oscillations of the revival signals. Owing to the relatively large CDN of CH3I (𝐵/𝐷~1.2 ∙106), these effects are observed already at 1.5Trev (~100 ps) as depicted by the black curve in Fig.2b. This distortion can be pre-compensated by the cubic-phase shaped pulse (with 𝑏̃ = 2 ∙107 fs3), depicted by the blue curve, with the typically single-cycle shape of the half-revival signal. At larger revival periods (longer delays), the CDN...

Figure 3 (a) Alignment signal induced by an unshaped 120fs excitation pulse in CO2 at 3/4 Trev and at (b) the 32Trev period showing severe multi-oscillatory signal shape. (c) Simulated CDN-free alignment signal at the 32Trev including the l- doubling contributions of CO2 at 293 K (blue) and 403 K (red). (d) Experimental revival signal at the 32Trev period of CO2 induced by a cubic phase shaped pulse at 293 K (blue) and 403 K (red). (e,f,g) enlarged view of selected regions marked by the dotted green rectangles in (d).

Fig.3d depicts the experimental alignment in the range 1380-1412 ps of CO2 induced by a cubic-phase-shaped pulse, designed to pre-compensate for the CDN effects at the 32Trev period. The blue and red curves were measured at 293 K and 403 K respectively and
